cataloging_subject.label: An Egerton Marriage Settlement, dated 10 Henry VI
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the anonymous Middle English document arranging the marriage of William Egerton and Margerie Egerton and associated property, dower, payment, and inheritance provisions. The document calls itself an indenture and bears a date formula equivalent to 20 March 1432, but William Beamont's surrounding 1864 analysis argues that it is spurious and probably a contemporary forgery; actual creation date and authenticity therefore remain unresolved. It is not Beamont's complete article, the article's authenticity argument, the physical document as an object, the 1864 plate, the U-M encoding, or the checked-in XML artifact. CME00127 retains Beamont's supplied article subtitle and editorial headnote as distinct paratext.

catalog_summary.title: An Egerton Marriage Settlement, dated 10 Henry VI
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous
catalog_summary.date: Dated 20 March 1432 (authenticity disputed); first known publication 1864
catalog_summary.region: Cheshire, England (documentary associations); scribe possibly Cheshire or the Welsh border

titles.value: An Egerton Marriage Settlement, dated 10 Henry VI
titles.supplied_by: William Beamont in the 1864 source article

assertion.notes: This established access title is Beamont's printed article subtitle on p. 183. It is not demonstrated to be a title written on the original document, and the encoding relocates it after PB N=197.

titles.value: Marriage settlement of William Egerton and Margerie Egerton

assertion.notes: A descriptive title identifying the intended spouses without deciding whether the deed is authentic.

titles.value: Thes endentures, made in the feste of Seint Cutbert, the yere of the regne of Kynge Henry the sext, aftur the conqueste the tente
Yeven the yere and day abovesaide

agents.name: Anonymous document maker
notes: No named drafter or scribe is established. The parties named in the instrument are not automatically its authors, and Beamont's forgery hypothesis does not identify a maker.

agents.name: William Beamont
notes: Author of the 1864 article, supplier of the modern title and headnote, and stated transcriber of the original document.

assertion.notes: Responsibility is anonymous at the textual-document layer; named covenanting parties are not converted into authors without evidence of drafting or inscription.
Beamont says the appended text is a copy of the settlement at length from the original then in Sir Philip Grey Egerton's possession.
The preferred title and headnote belong to Beamont's nineteenth-century editorial layer, not to the anonymous document.

date_statements.display: Dated 20 March 1432 (the Feast of St Cuthbert in 10 Henry VI); authenticity disputed

assertion.notes: The text directly supplies the regnal and feast-day formula, and Beamont converts it to 20 March 1432. Because his surrounding analysis argues that the instrument is spurious, the normalized date is a claimed execution date rather than a confirmed creation date.
Beamont's publication is the earliest item-specific attestation verified in this review; this is not a claim that no earlier reference exists.

This is the first publication identified in the sources reviewed, not an exhaustive proof that no earlier publication exists. Beamont presents the settlement as an original then in private possession and prints it at length.

places.label: Nantwich
notes: Named in the document as Wich Malbank or Wyche Mabank.

assertion.notes: The named Egerton parties and principal property references belong to Cheshire contexts. This association does not by itself prove where or when the surviving document was made.
The instrument provides for lands and tenements in Chester and other places.
The text names Wich Malbank, the historical name of Nantwich, as a location of settled lands.
Beamont says the spelling suggests a Cheshire scribe or a Welsh borderer. Cheshire is retained as one attributed possibility, not a confirmed place of copying or composition.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: Modern English in Beamont's supplied title and headnote is editorial paratext and is not promoted to a work-level language facet.

form_statements.description: A prose legal instrument framed by a brief prose editorial headnote in the encoded item.

assertion.notes: Genre describes the instrument's textual form and professed function without deciding its legal authenticity.
The instrument uses indenture, covenant, feoffment, remainder, obligation, warranty, and sealing formulas.

summaries.value: A Middle English indenture-form document arranging the intended marriage of William, son and heir of Philip Egerton, to Margerie, daughter of Ralph Egerton of the Wryne. It allocates clothing costs, directs feoffments and remainders in lands at Wich Malbank, Chester, and elsewhere, protects Alice Egerton's dower, specifies marriage payments and installments, imposes financial penalties for breach, and ends with a sealing formula. The document bears a date equivalent to 20 March 1432, but its authenticity and actual creation date are disputed.

CME00127 is a discontiguous selection from Beamont's article. It relocates the article subtitle printed on p. 183 as the XML HEAD, then encodes the p. 197 footnote headnote and the quoted settlement in separate footnote zones on pp. 197-199. Beamont's main analysis continues on those same pages, including the spurious-settlement conclusion at the top of p. 199, but is excluded; the encoding ends within p. 199 before the pedigree and does not include the photographic plate.

Beamont converts the document's date formula to 20 March 1432 but argues from genealogical and inquisition evidence that the settlement is spurious, while proposing that it may be a contemporary forgery connected with an actual later marriage between the named parties. This remains an attributed 1864 interpretation: the manifest neither validates the deed as an authentic 1432 legal instrument nor promotes Beamont's conclusion to settled modern consensus.

extent.description: One prose settlement in one DIV1 and one P, preceded by one prose editorial headnote in one P and a supplied HEAD. The body also contains three PB milestones, two italic '(sic)' markers, and four SUP elements. The p. 183 heading and pp. 197-199 prose are discontiguous source loci despite their continuous XML arrangement.

content_parts.label: Beamont's supplied title and headnote
content_parts.summary: The article subtitle and a footnote note saying that the following text is a copy at length from the original then in Sir Philip Grey Egerton's possession and that a one-quarter-size photographic facsimile accompanies the paper.

evidence.citation: General Corpus repository copy of CME/source/CME_phase_3/CME00127.xml at pinned CME commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.locator: IDG, TEXT, DIV1, HEAD, HEADNOTE, settlement P, PB N=197-199, italic HI, and SUP
evidence.summary: Primary evidence for the encoded title, Middle English text, named parties and places, date formula, covenants, financial and property provisions, sealing formula, typography, and XML structure.

evidence.citation: Parent-aware lxml audit of CME00127.xml and page-by-page collation against both complete 1864 scans.
evidence.summary: Confirmed 7,360 bytes; one DIV1 typed marriage settlement, one HEAD, one HEADNOTE, two intellectual P elements, three PB, two italic HI, and four SUP. Page collation established the relocated p. 183 subtitle, the spatial footnote zones on pp. 197-199, and the exact mid-page endpoint before the p. 199 pedigree.

evidence.citation: William Beamont, 'On Marriage Contracts, with Remarks on an Egerton Marriage Settlement, dated 10 Henry VI,' Journal of the Architectural, Archaeological, and Historic Society for the County, City, and Neighbourhood of Chester, old series 2 (1864): 183-202.
evidence.locator: subtitle p. 183; date/dialect/object discussion pp. 195-196; footnote headnote and transcription zones pp. 197-199; authenticity argument pp. 199-201
evidence.summary: Primary source for the supplied title, William Beamont's editorial and transcription responsibility, date conversion, Cheshire-or-Welsh-border scribe inference, 1864 possession, document and seal description, complete transcription, actual-marriage discussion, and attributed spurious/contemporary-forgery conclusion.

evidence.citation: Beamont's one-quarter-size photographic plate of the Egerton settlement, published between article pp. 196 and 197.
evidence.locator: ADS PDF p. 16; Internet Archive volume PDF p. 311
evidence.summary: Direct visual evidence for one long visible document surface reproduced in a single reduced monochrome plate; no reverse or other physical surfaces are shown.

evidence.citation: Archaeology Data Service article record 3204271 and Journal of the Chester Archaeological Society old-series volume 2 issue record 1147315.
evidence.summary: Official evidence for Beamont's authorship, exact article title, old-series volume 2, publication year 1864, pp. 183-202, DOI, and direct complete PDF.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse collection page and stable CME00127 identifier.
evidence.summary: Official collection evidence for public CME access, manual keying from public-domain editions, proofreading and editing, TEI-style markup, bulk XML availability, and No Copyright - United States framing; the stable identifier is CME00127.
notes: The exact item route was intermittently blocked to automated retrieval during review; the pinned repository copy remained available for complete textual inspection.

evidence.citation: Cheshire Archives and Local Studies, Estate and Family Records page and Egerton of Oulton estate collection DEO catalog route.
evidence.summary: Current institutional context for the Egerton of Oulton collection. Exact searches on 2026-07-13 found no item-level record for this settlement, so the evidence supports an unresolved current-holding question rather than a present repository attribution.

open_questions.question: What actual creation date and authenticity status should current scholarship assign to the settlement?
next_steps: Locate later editions, documentary studies, or a modern item-level catalog that evaluates Beamont's conclusion.
If the original is found, compare script, language, support, ink, indenture edge, seal area, arithmetic, parties, and land provisions with securely dated Egerton deeds.
Until then, retain 20 March 1432 as the document's claimed date and Beamont's forgery conclusion as an attributed interpretation.

open_questions.question: Where is the original document now, and can an exact current shelfmark, provenance chain, and complete reproduction be established?
next_steps: Ask Cheshire Archives about uncataloged or differently described Oulton-family deeds without assuming that collection DEO contains the item.
Trace Egerton family and Oulton Hall estate records after Sir Philip Grey Egerton's 1864 possession.
Request every surviving physical surface once the holder is identified; keep the 1864 plate as one-surface partial coverage.

notes: The preferred title is an 1864 supplied access title, not a demonstrated native heading on the original document.
The XML's PB N=197 precedes a HEAD copied from printed p. 183; page-break order therefore cannot be used as a literal source-page provenance claim.
Beamont's main analysis is interleaved with the footnote transcription on printed pp. 197-199. The XML includes only the footnote headnote and document text, ending mid-page before the pedigree.
The form and genre statements describe the document's textual and professed legal character without deciding its authenticity.
Modern English editorial framing is described at part level and does not make the cataloged historical work bilingual.
Sir Philip Grey Egerton's possession is securely stated for 1864 only; current holding and complete physical-object image coverage remain unknown.
